reviewQuestions: Why can't you cache availability data for long periods?
How would you cache search results when they depend on real-time availability?
What image sizes should Airbnb serve and how does responsive images work?
When a host changes their price, how quickly must the cache reflect this?
sessions.successCriteria: Can design a caching strategy that correctly handles cacheable vs real-time data.
sessions.content:
## Caching & Performance

### Multi-Layer Cache Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    Client[Client] --> CDN[CDN Layer - Images, Static Assets]
    CDN --> LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> App[App Server]
    App --> LocalCache[Local Cache - Config, Feature Flags]
    App --> Redis[Redis Cluster - Listing Data, Search Results]
    App --> DB[(PostgreSQL)]

    subgraph Image_Pipeline[Image Pipeline]
        Upload[Image Upload] --> Process[Image Processor - Resize, Compress]
        Process --> S3[S3 Origin]
        S3 --> CDN2[CDN Edge - Global PoPs]
    end
```

### What to Cache vs What Not to Cache

| Data | Cache? | TTL | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Listing metadata | Yes | 1 hour | Changes infrequently |
| Listing photos | Yes (CDN) | 24 hours | Immutable once uploaded |
| Search results | Yes | 5 min | Availability changes often |
| Real-time availability | No | - | Must be fresh for booking |
| User sessions | Yes | 30 min | Standard session caching |
| Pricing | Short | 15 min | Dynamic but not real-time |

### Redis Cache Implementation

```python
import redis
import json
from datetime import timedelta

redis_client = redis.Redis(host='redis-cluster', port=6379, db=0)

class ListingCache:
    PREFIX = "listing:"
    TTL = timedelta(hours=1)

    @staticmethod
    async def get_listing(listing_id: str) -> dict | None:
        cached = redis_client.get(f"{ListingCache.PREFIX}{listing_id}")
        if cached:
            return json.loads(cached)
        return None

    @staticmethod
    async def set_listing(listing_id: str, data: dict):
        redis_client.setex(
            f"{ListingCache.PREFIX}{listing_id}",
            ListingCache.TTL,
            json.dumps(data)
        )

    @staticmethod
    async def invalidate(listing_id: str):
        redis_client.delete(f"{ListingCache.PREFIX}{listing_id}")


class SearchCache:
    PREFIX = "search:"
    TTL = timedelta(minutes=5)

    @staticmethod
    def cache_key(params: dict) -> str:
        sorted_params = sorted(params.items())
        return f"{SearchCache.PREFIX}{hash(tuple(sorted_params))}"

    @staticmethod
    async def get_results(params: dict) -> list | None:
        cached = redis_client.get(SearchCache.cache_key(params))
        if cached:
            return json.loads(cached)
        return None
```

### Java Cache Implementation

```java
@Service
public class ListingCacheService {

    private final RedisTemplate<String, String> redis;
    private static final Duration TTL = Duration.ofHours(1);

    public Optional<Listing> getCachedListing(String listingId) {
        String key = "listing:" + listingId;
        String cached = redis.opsForValue().get(key);
        if (cached != null) {
            return Optional.of(objectMapper.readValue(cached, Listing.class));
        }
        return Optional.empty();
    }

    public void cacheListing(String listingId, Listing listing) {
        String key = "listing:" + listingId;
        redis.opsForValue().set(key, objectMapper.writeValueAsString(listing), TTL);
    }
}
```

### Image CDN Strategy

Airbnb serves images at multiple resolutions for different devices:

| Size | Dimensions | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Thumbnail | 300x200 | Search results grid |
| Medium | 720x480 | Listing card hover |
| Large | 1440x960 | Listing detail page |
| Original | Varies | Full-screen gallery |

Images are processed on upload:

```python
def process_listing_image(image_bytes: bytes, listing_id: str) -> dict:
    sizes = {
        "thumbnail": (300, 200),
        "medium": (720, 480),
        "large": (1440, 960),
    }
    urls = {}
    for name, dimensions in sizes.items():
        resized = resize_and_compress(image_bytes, dimensions)
        key = f"listings/{listing_id}/{name}_{uuid4()}.webp"
        s3.put_object(Bucket="airbnb-images", Key=key, Body=resized)
        urls[name] = f"https://cdn.airbnb.com/{key}"
    return urls
```

### Cache Consistency for Price Updates

When a host changes their price, the cache must reflect this quickly:

1. Host updates price via API
2. Write to PostgreSQL
3. Publish `listing.price_updated` event to Kafka
4. Cache invalidation consumer deletes the Redis cache entry
5. Next read triggers a cache miss and fetches fresh data

**Time to consistency:** ~2-5 seconds (acceptable for pricing)

For availability changes (bookings), skip the cache entirely and query PostgreSQL directly at booking time.

### Interview Tips

> **Tip:** A common interview question is "Can you cache search results?" The answer is nuanced: you can cache for identical queries (same location, dates, filters) with a short TTL (5 minutes), but the booking confirmation must always check real-time availability in the database. Stale search results are acceptable; stale booking confirmations are not.

sessions.title: Scalability & Global Architecture
sessions.paretoJustification: Airbnb operates globally with highly regional traffic — understanding geo-partitioning and multi-region architecture is essential for handling this scale.
objectives: Design horizontal scaling for search and booking services
Implement database sharding for listings and bookings
Handle multi-region deployment for global availability

activities.description: Design Elasticsearch cluster scaling: sharding by region, replicas for read throughput
Implement database sharding: listings by region, bookings by listing_id
Design multi-region deployment: US, EU, APAC with data locality
Handle cross-region bookings (US guest booking EU listing)

reviewQuestions: How would you shard the Elasticsearch index for listings?
Should booking data be sharded by guest or by listing?
How do you handle a guest in the US booking a listing in Europe?
What is data locality and why does it matter for Airbnb?
sessions.successCriteria: Can design multi-region architecture with proper sharding strategies.
sessions.content:
## Scalability & Global Architecture

### Elasticsearch Cluster Scaling

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph US_Cluster[US Region]
        US_ES1[ES Node 1 - Americas Listings]
        US_ES2[ES Node 2 - Americas Listings]
        US_ES3[ES Node 3 - Americas Listings]
    end
    subgraph EU_Cluster[EU Region]
        EU_ES1[ES Node 1 - Europe Listings]
        EU_ES2[ES Node 2 - Europe Listings]
        EU_ES3[ES Node 3 - Europe Listings]
    end
    subgraph APAC_Cluster[APAC Region]
        AP_ES1[ES Node 1 - APAC Listings]
        AP_ES2[ES Node 2 - APAC Listings]
        AP_ES3[ES Node 3 - APAC Listings]
    end
```

### Sharding Strategy

**Elasticsearch sharding - by region:**
- Each region has its own ES cluster
- Listings are indexed in the cluster closest to their physical location
- Cross-region searches (US guest searching EU) route to the EU cluster

**PostgreSQL sharding - by entity type:**

| Table | Shard Key | Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Listings | region_id | Geographic locality |
| Bookings | listing_id | Co-locate with listing data |
| Users | user_id | Hash-based consistent hashing |
| Reviews | listing_id | Co-locate with listing data |

```python
class ShardRouter:
    REGION_SHARDS = {
        "us-west": "shard-us-1",
        "us-east": "shard-us-2",
        "eu-west": "shard-eu-1",
        "eu-east": "shard-eu-2",
        "apac":    "shard-apac-1",
    }

    def get_listing_shard(self, listing_id: int) -> str:
        region = self.get_listing_region(listing_id)
        return self.REGION_SHARDS[region]

    def get_booking_shard(self, listing_id: int) -> str:
        return self.get_listing_shard(listing_id)

    def get_user_shard(self, user_id: int) -> str:
        shard_index = consistent_hash(user_id, num_shards=8)
        return f"shard-user-{shard_index}"
```

### Multi-Region Deployment

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph US[US Region]
        US_LB[LB] --> US_API[API Servers]
        US_API --> US_DB[(Primary DB)]
        US_API --> US_ES[(ES Cluster)]
    end
    subgraph EU[EU Region]
        EU_LB[LB] --> EU_API[API Servers]
        EU_API --> EU_DB[(Primary DB)]
        EU_API --> EU_ES[(ES Cluster)]
    end

    US_DB <-->|Async Replication| EU_DB
    DNS[Global DNS - GeoDNS] --> US_LB
    DNS --> EU_LB
```

**Data locality principle:** Most Airbnb searches are local (a guest in Paris searches for Paris listings), so routing to the nearest region serves most queries from local data.

### Cross-Region Booking

When a US guest books a listing in Europe:

1. Guest's request hits US API Gateway
2. US Gateway detects listing is in EU region
3. Request is forwarded to EU Booking Service (where the listing data lives)
4. EU Booking Service processes the transaction locally
5. Booking confirmation is replicated back to US for the guest's booking history

```java
@Service
public class CrossRegionBookingService {

    public Booking createBooking(BookingRequest request) {
        String listingRegion = regionResolver.getRegion(request.getListingId());
        String guestRegion = regionResolver.getRegion(request.getGuestId());

        if (!listingRegion.equals(guestRegion)) {
            return remoteBookingClient.createBooking(listingRegion, request);
        }
        return localBookingService.createBooking(request);
    }
}
```

### Horizontal Scaling Strategies

**Stateless API servers:** Auto-scale based on CPU/request count. Each server is identical.

**Search Service:** Scale Elasticsearch by adding replicas (not shards) for read throughput. More replicas = more concurrent search queries.

**Booking Service:** Does not need many instances (23 QPS average). Scale for reliability (multiple instances for failover), not throughput.

### Capacity Planning

| Component | Baseline | Peak (2x) | Growth/Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| API servers | 50 instances | 100 instances | +30% |
| ES cluster | 9 nodes (3 per region) | 18 nodes | +40% |
| PostgreSQL | 8 shards | 8 shards | Re-shard at 80% capacity |
| Redis | 6 nodes (cluster) | 6 nodes | +20% |

### Interview Tips

> **Tip:** When discussing multi-region, emphasize **data locality** - most Airbnb queries are geographically local. A guest in Tokyo searches Tokyo listings, which are stored in the APAC cluster. Cross-region requests are the exception, not the rule. This simplifies the architecture significantly compared to a globally distributed system.

sessions.title: Reliability, Trust & Safety
sessions.paretoJustification: Airbnb handles money and physical safety — reliability of bookings, fraud detection, and trust systems are non-negotiable for a marketplace platform.
objectives: Design payment reliability with escrow pattern
Implement fraud detection for fake listings and scam guests
Design review system with anti-gaming measures

activities.description: Design payment flow: guest pays -> escrow -> host paid after check-in + 24h
Implement fraud detection: fake listing detection, identity verification
Design review system: mutual blind reviews, anti-gaming (delayed reveal)
Define SLOs and monitoring for booking reliability

reviewQuestions: Why does Airbnb use an escrow pattern for payments?
How do mutual blind reviews prevent review manipulation?
What signals would you use to detect a fake listing?
What is the SLO for booking confirmation success rate?
sessions.successCriteria: Can design payment escrow, fraud detection, and review reliability systems.
sessions.content:
## Reliability, Trust & Safety

### Payment Flow with Escrow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Guest
    participant BS as Booking Service
    participant PS as Payment Service
    participant Escrow as Escrow Account
    participant Host

    Guest->>BS: Book listing ($661)
    BS->>PS: Charge guest
    PS->>Escrow: Hold $661
    PS-->>Guest: Payment confirmed

    Note over Escrow: Funds held until check-in + 24h

    Guest->>BS: Check in
    Note over Escrow: Wait 24 hours

    Escrow->>Host: Release $579 (minus fees)
    Escrow->>PS: Airbnb fee: $82
```

The escrow pattern protects both parties:
- **Guest:** Money is refundable if the listing does not match the description
- **Host:** Guaranteed payment once the guest checks in
- **Airbnb:** Collects service fees and mediates disputes

### Idempotent Payment Processing

```python
class PaymentService:
    def charge_guest(self, booking_id: str, amount, idempotency_key: str):
        # Check if this payment was already processed
        existing = self.db.get_payment_by_idempotency_key(idempotency_key)
        if existing:
            return existing  # Return previous result, do not charge again

        try:
            charge = stripe.Charge.create(
                amount=int(amount * 100),
                currency="usd",
                customer=self.get_stripe_customer(booking_id),
                idempotency_key=idempotency_key,
            )
            result = PaymentResult(status="success", charge_id=charge.id)
        except stripe.error.CardError as e:
            result = PaymentResult(status="failed", error=str(e))

        self.db.save_payment(booking_id, idempotency_key, result)
        return result
```

### Fraud Detection

**Fake Listing Detection Signals:**
- Photos reverse-image-searched and found on other sites
- Listing price significantly below market rate
- New host with no verification
- Description copied from another listing
- Host unresponsive to messages

**Scam Guest Detection:**
- Multiple cancellations after check-in
- Chargebacks on previous bookings
- Account created with disposable email
- IP geolocation mismatch with stated location

```python
class FraudDetector:
    def score_listing(self, listing) -> float:
        score = 0.0

        if listing.price < market_average(listing.location) * 0.4:
            score += 0.3  # Suspiciously cheap

        if not listing.host.identity_verified:
            score += 0.2

        if listing.host.account_age_days < 7:
            score += 0.15

        if listing.photo_count < 3:
            score += 0.1

        if reverse_image_search(listing.photos):
            score += 0.4  # Stolen photos

        return min(score, 1.0)
```

### Review System Design

Airbnb uses **mutual blind reviews** to prevent retaliation:

1. Both guest and host write reviews independently
2. Reviews are hidden until both submit (or 14-day deadline passes)
3. Neither party can see the other's review before submitting
4. After both submit, reviews are revealed simultaneously

```java
@Service
public class ReviewService {

    public Review submitReview(Long bookingId, Long authorId, ReviewInput input) {
        Booking booking = bookingRepository.findById(bookingId);
        validateReviewEligibility(booking, authorId);

        Review review = new Review();
        review.setBookingId(bookingId);
        review.setAuthorId(authorId);
        review.setRating(input.getRating());
        review.setText(input.getText());
        review.setVisible(false);

        reviewRepository.save(review);

        // Check if both reviews are in - if so, reveal both
        List<Review> bookingReviews = reviewRepository.findByBookingId(bookingId);
        if (bookingReviews.size() == 2) {
            bookingReviews.forEach(r -> r.setVisible(true));
            reviewRepository.saveAll(bookingReviews);
        }

        return review;
    }
}
```

### SLOs and Monitoring

| Service | SLO | Error Budget (30 days) |
|---|---|---|
| Search availability | 99.95% | 21.6 min downtime |
| Booking success rate | 99.99% | 4.3 min downtime |
| Payment processing | 99.99% | 4.3 min downtime |
| Image serving (CDN) | 99.99% | 4.3 min downtime |

**Key metrics to monitor:**
- Search latency p50, p95, p99
- Booking conversion rate (search-to-book)
- Payment failure rate
- Review submission rate
- Host response time
- Availability calendar sync lag (ES vs PostgreSQL)

### Graceful Degradation

When services fail, degrade gracefully:

| Failure | Degraded Behavior |
|---|---|
| Elasticsearch down | Serve cached search results (stale but functional) |
| Payment service down | Allow reservations, retry payment async |
| Review service down | Hide reviews section, booking still works |
| Image CDN down | Show placeholder images |
| Messaging down | Queue messages, deliver when restored |

### Interview Tips

> **Tip:** The escrow payment pattern is unique to marketplaces. Be ready to explain the full lifecycle: charge guest at booking -> hold in escrow -> release to host after check-in + 24h buffer. The 24h buffer exists so guests can report issues before the host receives payment.

quizBank.question: How does Airbnb prevent double bookings for the same listing and dates?
options: Eventual consistency — last write wins
Pessimistic locking: SELECT FOR UPDATE on the availability rows during the booking transaction
Optimistic locking with no retries
A global lock on all listings
quizBank.correctAnswer: Pessimistic locking: SELECT FOR UPDATE on the availability rows during the booking transaction
quizBank.explanation: To prevent double bookings, Airbnb uses pessimistic locking. When a guest initiates a booking, the system acquires a row-level lock (SELECT FOR UPDATE) on the availability records for those dates. If another booking attempt arrives for the same dates, it blocks until the first transaction completes. This ensures only one booking can succeed. Optimistic locking (version check) is an alternative but requires retry logic.

quizBank.question: What type of search index does Airbnb primarily use for listing discovery?
options: MySQL full-text search
Elasticsearch with geo_point fields
MongoDB Atlas Search
Redis Search
quizBank.correctAnswer: Elasticsearch with geo_point fields
quizBank.explanation: Airbnb uses Elasticsearch for its search infrastructure. Elasticsearch supports geo_point fields for geospatial queries (find listings within a bounding box or radius), nested objects for availability, and full-text search for listing descriptions. Its distributed architecture handles Airbnb's 100M+ daily searches.

quizBank.question: Why does Airbnb use a 2-phase booking process (reserve then confirm)?
options: To save server resources
The reservation hold prevents the listing from being double-booked while the guest completes payment, avoiding lost bookings due to payment delays
To comply with hotel industry regulations
To give hosts time to review the guest
quizBank.correctAnswer: The reservation hold prevents the listing from being double-booked while the guest completes payment, avoiding lost bookings due to payment delays
quizBank.explanation: The 2-phase process solves a key problem: payment processing takes 5-30 seconds and can fail. Without a hold, two guests could simultaneously try to book the same dates — both see it as available, but only one payment succeeds. The reservation hold (typically 10-15 minutes) temporarily blocks those dates, giving the guest time to complete payment without risk of losing the booking.

quizBank.question: What is the escrow payment pattern and why does Airbnb use it?
options: Instant payment to the host upon booking
Guest's payment is held by Airbnb and released to the host 24 hours after check-in, protecting both parties
Payment is split equally between Airbnb and the host
The guest pays after the stay is complete
quizBank.correctAnswer: Guest's payment is held by Airbnb and released to the host 24 hours after check-in, protecting both parties
quizBank.explanation: In the escrow pattern, Airbnb charges the guest at booking time but holds the funds. The host receives payment 24 hours after the guest checks in. This protects guests (refund if listing is misrepresented) and hosts (guaranteed payment for legitimate stays). It also gives Airbnb time to handle disputes.

quizBank.question: How would you efficiently query 'available listings in Paris for June 1-7, 2+ bedrooms, under $150/night'?
quizBank.explanation: **Pipeline approach:** (1) Elasticsearch geo-query: filter listings within Paris bounding box using geo_bounding_box. (2) Attribute filter: bedrooms >= 2 AND price <= 150 (Elasticsearch term/range queries). (3) Availability check: either pre-indexed in ES (availability as nested date ranges) or post-filter against availability service. (4) Rank by quality score. The availability check is the hard part — options: (a) Denormalize availability into Elasticsearch (fast but complex to keep in sync), (b) Two-phase: ES returns candidate listings, then batch-check availability against the booking database. Option (b) is simpler but adds latency. Airbnb uses a hybrid: popular date ranges are pre-indexed, long-tail queries do the two-phase check.

quizBank.question: What signals does Airbnb use to rank search results?
options: Only the listing price (cheapest first)
Quality score (reviews, photos, response rate), relevance (location match, amenity match), personalization (user's past booking patterns), and conversion probability
Random ordering to give all hosts equal visibility
Only the number of reviews
quizBank.correctAnswer: Quality score (reviews, photos, response rate), relevance (location match, amenity match), personalization (user's past booking patterns), and conversion probability
quizBank.explanation: Airbnb's ranking model combines multiple signals: (1) Quality: average review score, number of reviews, professional photos, host response rate, Superhost status. (2) Relevance: how well the listing matches the query (location, dates, guest count, amenities). (3) Personalization: user's past booking history, price sensitivity, preferred neighborhoods. (4) Conversion probability: ML model predicting likelihood of booking. This is a learning-to-rank model trained on historical booking data.

quizBank.question: Approximately how many active listings does Airbnb have worldwide?
options: 2 million
7 million+
50 million
quizBank.correctAnswer: 7 million+
quizBank.explanation: Airbnb has over 7 million active listings across 100,000+ cities in 220+ countries. This scale means the search index is large but manageable (compared to billions of web pages). The main challenge is not index size but query complexity — each search combines geo-spatial, temporal (availability), and attribute filters.

quizBank.question: Why are mutual blind reviews important for Airbnb's trust system?
options: They are faster to write
Neither party can see the other's review until both have submitted (or the window expires), preventing retaliatory reviews
They save storage space
They are required by law in some countries
quizBank.correctAnswer: Neither party can see the other's review until both have submitted (or the window expires), preventing retaliatory reviews
quizBank.explanation: In mutual blind reviews, the guest's review of the host and the host's review of the guest are both hidden until both are submitted (or a 14-day window expires). This prevents retaliatory reviews — a host won't give a guest a bad review just because the guest gave them a bad one, since they can't see it yet. This leads to more honest reviews and stronger trust in the platform.

quizBank.question: How would you design the availability calendar to efficiently answer 'is this listing available from date X to date Y?'
quizBank.explanation: **Two main approaches:** (1) **Date-per-row model:** Table: availability(listing_id, date, status). One row per date per listing. Query: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM availability WHERE listing_id=X AND date BETWEEN X AND Y AND status='blocked'. If count > 0, not available. Simple but creates many rows (7M listings * 365 days = 2.5B rows/year). (2) **Range model:** Table: bookings(listing_id, start_date, end_date). Query: SELECT 1 FROM bookings WHERE listing_id=X AND start_date < Y AND end_date > X. If any row returned, there is an overlap (not available). Fewer rows but range overlap queries need careful indexing. **Airbnb's approach:** Hybrid — use the range model for bookings and a separate blocked_dates table for host-blocked dates. Index on (listing_id, start_date, end_date). Cache hot listings' availability in Redis as a bitmap (1 bit per day for the next 365 days).

quizBank.question: What is the main challenge of sharding the listings database by geographic region?
options: Regions have unequal numbers of listings, creating hotspots (e.g., Paris has 65K+ listings while some regions have hundreds)
Geographic regions do not change
All queries are single-region
There is no challenge — geographic sharding is always balanced
quizBank.correctAnswer: Regions have unequal numbers of listings, creating hotspots (e.g., Paris has 65K+ listings while some regions have hundreds)
quizBank.explanation: Geographic sharding creates unbalanced partitions. Paris alone has 65,000+ listings while entire countries may have only a few hundred. Solutions: (1) Use fine-grained geo-cells (not countries) as shard keys, (2) Split large cities across multiple shards, (3) Use consistent hashing with virtual nodes to rebalance. Also, cross-region search queries (e.g., 'listings in Southern France') may span multiple shards, requiring scatter-gather queries.

cheatSheet:
# Design Airbnb - Cheat Sheet

## Scale Numbers
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Active listings | 7M+ |
| Countries | 220+ |
| Cities | 100K+ |
| Searches/day | ~100M |
| Bookings/day | ~2M |
| Images stored | ~140M (7M * 20 avg) |

## Core Components
- **Search Service** - Elasticsearch + geo + availability
- **Listing Service** - CRUD for listings, calendar management
- **Booking Service** - reservation, payment, confirmation
- **Pricing Service** - dynamic pricing, smart pricing tool
- **Review Service** - mutual blind reviews
- **Messaging Service** - host-guest communication
- **Payment Service** - escrow, payouts, refunds
- **Trust Service** - identity verification, fraud detection

## Technology Choices
| Component | Technology |
|-----------|-----------|
| Search | Elasticsearch (geo_point + nested) |
| Listing DB | MySQL (sharded by region) |
| Booking DB | MySQL (SERIALIZABLE isolation) |
| Cache | Redis + Memcached |
| Image Storage | S3 + CloudFront CDN |
| Message Queue | Kafka |
| ML Ranking | TensorFlow Serving |

## Double-Booking Prevention
```sql
BEGIN;
SELECT * FROM availability
WHERE listing_id = ? AND date BETWEEN ? AND ?
FOR UPDATE;
-- Check all dates are available
INSERT INTO bookings (listing_id, guest_id, check_in, check_out, status)
VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, 'confirmed');
UPDATE availability SET status = 'booked'
WHERE listing_id = ? AND date BETWEEN ? AND ?;
COMMIT;
```

## Search Pipeline
1. Geo filter (bounding box) -> ~10K candidates
2. Date availability filter -> ~2K candidates
3. Attribute filter (price, bedrooms, amenities) -> ~500
4. ML ranking (quality, relevance, personalization) -> top 20
5. Diversity injection -> final 20 results

## Interview Tips
1. Clarify: booking marketplace vs hotel chain (two-sided)
2. Emphasize search pipeline complexity (geo + dates + attrs)
3. Double-booking prevention is THE key transaction discussion
4. Mention: escrow payments, blind reviews, fraud detection
5. Deep dive: search ranking or availability calendar

plan.overview: A comprehensive case study on designing WhatsApp — the world's most popular messaging platform with 2B+ users sending 100B+ messages daily. resources.title: System Design Interview - Alex Xu Ch.12

reviewQuestions: How many messages does WhatsApp handle per second on average?
Why can WhatsApp handle 2B users with relatively few servers?
What is the storage growth rate for 100B messages/day?
Why is message delivery latency (< 200ms) a hard requirement?
sessions.successCriteria: Can calculate message QPS and explain why Erlang/BEAM enables WhatsApp's efficiency.
sessions.content:
## Session 1: Requirements & Back-of-Envelope Estimation

```mermaid
graph LR
    U[2B Users] --> C[500M Concurrent]
    C --> M[100B msgs/day]
    M --> S[~1.15M msgs/sec]
    S --> ST[~80 TB/day storage]
```

### Interview Opening

**Interviewer:** "Design WhatsApp."

**Candidate:** "Let me start by clarifying scope. Are we designing 1-on-1 chat, group chat, or both?"

**Interviewer:** "Both. Focus on messaging -- no voice/video calls."

**Candidate:** "What about media? Text only or images/videos/documents too?"

**Interviewer:** "Text and media. Focus on delivery guarantees."

**Candidate:** "Any scale target?"

**Interviewer:** "WhatsApp scale -- 2 billion users."

### Functional Requirements

- **1:1 messaging** with real-time delivery
- **Group messaging** (up to 1024 members)
- **Media sharing**: images, videos, documents (up to 100MB)
- **Read receipts**: sent (single check), delivered (double check), read (blue checks)
- **Online/last-seen status**
- **Offline message queuing**: messages delivered when user reconnects
- **End-to-end encryption** (all messages)

### Non-Functional Requirements

- **Latency**: < 200ms delivery when both users are online
- **Availability**: 99.99% -- messages must never be lost
- **Durability**: messages stored until delivered (then deleted from server)
- **Ordering**: guaranteed per-conversation
- **Scale**: 2B registered users, 500M concurrent connections

### Back-of-Envelope Estimation

| Metric | Calculation | Result |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| Daily messages | 2B users x 50 msgs/user | **100B messages/day** |
| Messages/sec | 100B / 86,400 | **~1.15M msgs/sec** |
| Concurrent connections | 25% of 2B | **500M connections** |
| Avg message size | 100 bytes text | 100 bytes |
| Daily storage (text) | 100B x 100 bytes | **~10 TB/day** |
| Daily storage (with media) | ~80 TB/day | **~80 TB/day** |
| Bandwidth | 80 TB / 86,400 | **~7.5 Gbps** |

### WhatsApp Legendary Efficiency

WhatsApp famously supported 900M users with only ~50 engineers. Key factor: **Erlang/OTP**. Each Erlang process uses ~2KB of memory, supporting millions of concurrent connections per server.

```python
# Back-of-envelope calculation
daily_users = 2_000_000_000
msgs_per_user = 50
daily_messages = daily_users * msgs_per_user  # 100B

msgs_per_sec = daily_messages / 86400  # ~1.15M
concurrent_connections = daily_users * 0.25  # 500M

# Connections per server (Erlang can handle 2M+ per server)
servers_needed = concurrent_connections / 2_000_000  # ~250 servers
print(f"Servers for connections: {servers_needed}")
```

### Interview Tip
> Always start with requirements clarification. At WhatsApp scale, the difference between "text only" and "text + media" is an 8x storage difference. Establishing constraints early shows maturity.

sessions.title: High-Level Architecture
sessions.paretoJustification: WhatsApp's architecture is deceptively simple at a high level — the complexity lies in the connection management and message routing layers.
objectives: Design the high-level architecture: clients, gateway, chat servers, storage
Understand the role of WebSocket/MQTT for persistent connections
Design the message routing layer

reviewQuestions: Why does WhatsApp use persistent connections instead of HTTP polling?

sessions.successCriteria: Can design the connection layer and explain message routing between chat servers.
sessions.content:
## Session 2: High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Clients
        A[Phone A]
        B[Phone B]
        C[Phone C]
    end
    subgraph Gateway Layer
        GW1[WebSocket Gateway 1]
        GW2[WebSocket Gateway 2]
    end
    subgraph Services
        CS[Chat Service]
        GS[Group Service]
        PS[Presence Service]
        MS[Media Service]
    end
    subgraph Storage
        MQ[Message Queue - Kafka]
        DB[(Message Store - HBase)]
        REDIS[(Redis - Sessions)]
        S3[Object Store - Media]
    end
    A <-->|WebSocket| GW1
    B <-->|WebSocket| GW1
    C <-->|WebSocket| GW2
    GW1 --> CS
    GW2 --> CS
    CS --> MQ
    CS --> DB
    CS --> REDIS
    CS --> GS
    GS --> MQ
    PS --> REDIS
    MS --> S3
```

### Why WebSocket?

| Protocol | Latency | Bidirectional | Connection Cost |
|----------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| HTTP Polling | High (1-30s) | No | High bandwidth waste |
| Long Polling | Medium | No | Moderate |
| **WebSocket** | **Low (<50ms)** | **Yes** | **One TCP connection** |
| MQTT | Low | Yes | Lightweight (IoT-friendly) |

WhatsApp originally used **XMPP** (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol), then moved to a custom protocol. For interviews, WebSocket is the standard answer.

### Connection Management

When a client connects, it establishes a WebSocket connection to a Gateway server. The Gateway maintains a mapping in Redis:

```python
# Connection registry in Redis
class ConnectionRegistry:
    def __init__(self, redis_client):
        self.redis = redis_client

    def register(self, user_id: str, gateway_id: str, conn_id: str):
        self.redis.hset(f"user:{user_id}:connections",
                       mapping={
                           "gateway_id": gateway_id,
                           "conn_id": conn_id,
                           "connected_at": int(time.time())
                       })
        self.redis.sadd(f"gateway:{gateway_id}:users", user_id)

    def lookup(self, user_id: str) -> dict:
        return self.redis.hgetall(f"user:{user_id}:connections")

    def unregister(self, user_id: str, gateway_id: str):
        self.redis.delete(f"user:{user_id}:connections")
        self.redis.srem(f"gateway:{gateway_id}:users", user_id)
```

### Message Routing Flow

1. **User A** sends message to **User B**
2. A gateway receives message, forwards to **Chat Service**
3. Chat Service looks up B gateway in Redis
4. If B is online: route to B gateway, deliver via WebSocket
5. If B is offline: store in **offline message queue**, deliver on reconnect

```java
// Java - Message routing service
public class MessageRouter {
    private final ConnectionRegistry registry;
    private final MessageQueue offlineQueue;
    private final GatewayClient gatewayClient;

    public void routeMessage(Message message) {
        String recipientId = message.getRecipientId();
        ConnectionInfo conn = registry.lookup(recipientId);

        if (conn != null && conn.isActive()) {
            // User is online - route to their gateway
            gatewayClient.deliver(conn.getGatewayId(), message);
        } else {
            // User is offline - queue for later delivery
            offlineQueue.enqueue(recipientId, message);
        }

        // Always persist the message
        messageStore.save(message);
    }
}
```

### Interview Tip
> Emphasize the separation of **connection management** (Gateway) from **business logic** (Chat Service). This enables independent scaling -- you might need 500 Gateways for connections but only 50 Chat Service instances for processing.

sessions.content:
## Session 3: Database Design & Message Storage

### Schema Design

```mermaid
erDiagram
    USERS {
        string user_id PK
        string phone_number UK
        string display_name
        string profile_pic_url
        timestamp created_at
    }
    CONVERSATIONS {
        string conversation_id PK
        string type
        timestamp created_at
    }
    CONVERSATION_MEMBERS {
        string conversation_id FK
        string user_id FK
        string role
        timestamp joined_at
    }
    MESSAGES {
        string message_id PK
        string conversation_id FK
        string sender_id FK
        string type
        bytes encrypted_content
        timestamp sent_at
        int sequence_number
    }
    MESSAGE_STATUS {
        string message_id FK
        string user_id FK
        string status
        timestamp updated_at
    }
    USERS ||--o{ CONVERSATION_MEMBERS : joins
    CONVERSATIONS ||--o{ CONVERSATION_MEMBERS : has
    CONVERSATIONS ||--o{ MESSAGES : contains
    MESSAGES ||--o{ MESSAGE_STATUS : tracks
```

### Storage Model: Per-User Inbox vs Per-Conversation

| Approach | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|------|
| **Per-conversation** | Simple queries, natural ordering | Fan-out on read for multi-device |
| **Per-user inbox** | Fast reads per user | Write amplification in groups |
| **Hybrid (WhatsApp)** | Best of both | More complexity |

WhatsApp uses a **write-through per-conversation** model: messages are stored by conversation, but each user has an inbox pointer tracking which messages they have received.

### Choosing the Right Database

```python
# Message storage with Cassandra (wide-column store)
# Partition key: conversation_id
# Clustering key: message_timestamp (descending for recent-first)

CREATE_TABLE_CQL = """
CREATE TABLE messages (
    conversation_id UUID,
    message_id TIMEUUID,
    sender_id UUID,
    message_type TEXT,
    encrypted_content BLOB,
    sent_at TIMESTAMP,
    PRIMARY KEY (conversation_id, message_id)
) WITH CLUSTERING ORDER BY (message_id DESC)
  AND default_time_to_live = 2592000;
"""

# Why Cassandra?
# 1. Write-optimized (LSM tree) -- perfect for 1.15M writes/sec
# 2. Partition by conversation_id -- all messages in one partition
# 3. Time-ordered within partition -- efficient pagination
# 4. TTL support -- auto-expire delivered messages
```

### Offline Message Queue

When users are offline, messages queue in a separate store and flush on reconnect:

```java
// Java - Offline message queue using Redis sorted sets
public class OfflineMessageQueue {
    private final RedisTemplate<String, String> redis;

    public void enqueue(String userId, Message message) {
        String key = "offline:" + userId;
        // Score = timestamp for ordering
        redis.opsForZSet().add(key,
            serialize(message),
            message.getSentAt().toEpochMilli());
        // Expire after 30 days
        redis.expire(key, Duration.ofDays(30));
    }

    public List<Message> drainQueue(String userId) {
        String key = "offline:" + userId;
        Set<String> messages = redis.opsForZSet()
            .range(key, 0, -1);
        redis.delete(key);  // Atomic drain
        return messages.stream()
            .map(this::deserialize)
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
    }
}
```

### Message ID Generation

Messages need globally unique, time-ordered IDs:

```python
# Snowflake-style ID generation
# | 41 bits timestamp | 10 bits machine_id | 12 bits sequence |
class MessageIdGenerator:
    EPOCH = 1609459200000  # 2021-01-01

    def __init__(self, machine_id: int):
        self.machine_id = machine_id & 0x3FF  # 10 bits
        self.sequence = 0
        self.last_timestamp = -1

    def next_id(self) -> int:
        ts = int(time.time() * 1000) - self.EPOCH
        if ts == self.last_timestamp:
            self.sequence = (self.sequence + 1) & 0xFFF
            if self.sequence == 0:
                ts = self._wait_next_ms(ts)
        else:
            self.sequence = 0
        self.last_timestamp = ts
        return (ts << 22) | (self.machine_id << 12) | self.sequence
```

### Interview Tip
> Mention that WhatsApp **deletes messages from server** after delivery -- the server is just a relay. This is a key design difference from Slack/Telegram that store everything permanently. It dramatically reduces storage requirements.

sessions.successCriteria: Can explain E2EE at a high level and describe the key exchange process.
sessions.content:
## Session 5: End-to-End Encryption & Security

### The Signal Protocol (Double Ratchet)

WhatsApp uses the **Signal Protocol** for end-to-end encryption. The server never sees plaintext messages -- it only relays encrypted blobs.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Alice
    participant S as Server (Key Store)
    participant B as Bob

    Note over A: Generate identity key pair
    Note over B: Generate identity key pair
    A->>S: Register public identity key + signed pre-key + one-time pre-keys
    B->>S: Register public identity key + signed pre-key + one-time pre-keys

    Note over A: Alice wants to message Bob
    A->>S: Request Bob key bundle
    S->>A: Bob public keys
    Note over A: X3DH key agreement - shared secret
    Note over A: Encrypt message with shared secret
    A->>S: Encrypted message
    S->>B: Encrypted message
    Note over B: Derive same shared secret
    Note over B: Decrypt message
```

### Key Types in Signal Protocol

| Key Type | Purpose | Lifetime |
|----------|---------|----------|
| **Identity Key** | Long-term user identity | Permanent |
| **Signed Pre-Key** | Medium-term, signed by identity key | Rotated weekly |
| **One-Time Pre-Keys** | Prevent replay attacks | Single use |
| **Session Key** | Encrypts actual messages | Per message (ratcheted) |

### X3DH Key Exchange

```python
# Simplified X3DH (Extended Triple Diffie-Hellman)
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric.x25519 import (
    X25519PrivateKey, X25519PublicKey
)
import hashlib

class X3DHKeyExchange:
    def initiate_session(self,
                         my_identity_key,
                         their_identity_pub,
                         their_signed_prekey,
                         their_one_time_prekey,
                         my_ephemeral_key) -> bytes:
        # Compute shared secret from 4 DH exchanges:
        # DH1: my_identity x their_signed_prekey
        # DH2: my_ephemeral x their_identity
        # DH3: my_ephemeral x their_signed_prekey
        # DH4: my_ephemeral x their_one_time_prekey
        dh1 = my_identity_key.exchange(their_signed_prekey)
        dh2 = my_ephemeral_key.exchange(their_identity_pub)
        dh3 = my_ephemeral_key.exchange(their_signed_prekey)
        dh4 = my_ephemeral_key.exchange(their_one_time_prekey)

        # KDF to derive shared secret
        master = dh1 + dh2 + dh3 + dh4
        return hashlib.sha256(master).digest()
```

### Double Ratchet: Forward Secrecy

The Double Ratchet ensures that even if a session key is compromised, past and future messages remain secure:

```java
// Java - Simplified Double Ratchet concept
public class DoubleRatchet {
    private byte[] rootKey;
    private byte[] chainKey;
    private int messageNumber = 0;

    // Each message derives a new message key from the chain key.
    // The chain key itself ratchets forward.
    // Old keys cannot derive new keys (forward secrecy).
    public byte[] ratchetMessageKey() {
        byte[] messageKey = HKDF.deriveKey(chainKey, "msg_key".getBytes());
        chainKey = HKDF.deriveKey(chainKey, "chain_ratchet".getBytes());
        messageNumber++;
        return messageKey;
    }

    // On DH ratchet step (when other party responds):
    public void dhRatchetStep(byte[] newDHSecret) {
        byte[] derived = HKDF.deriveKey(rootKey, newDHSecret);
        this.rootKey = Arrays.copyOfRange(derived, 0, 32);
        this.chainKey = Arrays.copyOfRange(derived, 32, 64);
        this.messageNumber = 0;
    }
}
```

### Group Encryption: Sender Keys

For groups, WhatsApp uses **Sender Keys** to avoid NxN key exchanges:

1. Each member generates a **sender key** and distributes it to all group members (encrypted 1-on-1)
2. When sending a group message, encrypt once with sender key
3. All recipients decrypt with that sender key

This means: **1 encryption per message** instead of N encryptions.

### Server-Side Implications

```python
# The server stores ONLY encrypted blobs
class EncryptedMessageStore:
    def store(self, message_id: str, encrypted_payload: bytes, metadata: dict):
        # Server stores:
        # - encrypted_payload: opaque bytes (cannot decrypt)
        # - metadata: sender_id, recipient_id, timestamp, message_type
        #
        # Server CANNOT see: message text, media content
        # Server CAN see: who messages whom, when, message size
        self.db.insert({
            "id": message_id,
            "payload": encrypted_payload,
            "sender": metadata["sender_id"],
            "recipient": metadata["recipient_id"],
            "timestamp": metadata["timestamp"],
            "size": len(encrypted_payload)
        })
```

### Interview Tip
> You do not need to implement the full Signal Protocol in an interview. Show you understand: (1) the server is a dumb relay that cannot read messages, (2) forward secrecy via key ratcheting, (3) the trade-off in group encryption between sender keys (efficient) and pairwise (more secure).

quizBank.question: Approximately how many messages does WhatsApp handle per day?
options: 1 billion
10 billion
100 billion+
1 trillion
quizBank.correctAnswer: 100 billion+
quizBank.explanation: WhatsApp processes over 100 billion messages per day across 2 billion users. This translates to approximately 1.15 million messages per second on average, with peak rates significantly higher.

quizBank.question: What programming language/platform powers WhatsApp's backend?
options: Java on JVM
Go
Erlang on BEAM VM
C++ with custom runtime
quizBank.correctAnswer: Erlang on BEAM VM
quizBank.explanation: WhatsApp is built on Erlang/BEAM VM. Erlang's lightweight processes (not OS threads) enable handling millions of concurrent connections per server. In 2012, WhatsApp famously handled 2 million connections on a single server with 2 CPUs. The BEAM VM's preemptive scheduling and fault tolerance (let-it-crash philosophy) make it ideal for messaging.

quizBank.question: What encryption protocol does WhatsApp use for end-to-end encryption?
options: AES-256 only
Signal Protocol (Double Ratchet)
TLS 1.3
quizBank.correctAnswer: Signal Protocol (Double Ratchet)
quizBank.explanation: WhatsApp uses the Signal Protocol (developed by Open Whisper Systems) for end-to-end encryption. The Double Ratchet algorithm provides forward secrecy — if a key is compromised, past messages remain secure because each message uses a different key. This means even WhatsApp's servers cannot decrypt messages.

quizBank.question: In WhatsApp's message delivery flow, what do the grey and blue ticks represent?
options: Grey = sent to server, Blue = sent to recipient
One grey tick = sent to server, Two grey ticks = delivered to recipient's device, Two blue ticks = read by recipient
Grey = unencrypted, Blue = encrypted
Grey = text message, Blue = media message
quizBank.correctAnswer: One grey tick = sent to server, Two grey ticks = delivered to recipient's device, Two blue ticks = read by recipient
quizBank.explanation: WhatsApp's tick system maps to the ACK flow: (1) One grey tick: message received by WhatsApp server (server ACK). (2) Two grey ticks: message delivered to recipient's device (delivery ACK). (3) Two blue ticks: recipient opened the chat and read the message (read ACK). This provides sender visibility into the delivery pipeline.

quizBank.question: Why can WhatsApp handle 2 million connections on a single server?
options: They use expensive server hardware
Erlang's lightweight processes use ~2KB each (vs 1MB per OS thread), enabling millions of concurrent processes
They use HTTP/2 multiplexing
Each server has 128 CPU cores
quizBank.correctAnswer: Erlang's lightweight processes use ~2KB each (vs 1MB per OS thread), enabling millions of concurrent processes
quizBank.explanation: Erlang creates lightweight 'processes' (not OS threads) that use approximately 2KB of memory each. A server with 64GB RAM can create millions of these processes. Each WebSocket connection is handled by one Erlang process. The BEAM VM schedules these processes preemptively across CPU cores with minimal context-switching overhead. Compare: a Java thread uses ~1MB stack, so the same server could only handle ~64K threads.

quizBank.question: How does WhatsApp handle messages for offline users?
options: Messages are dropped and the sender is notified
Messages are stored in a persistent queue on the server and delivered when the recipient reconnects
Messages are sent to the recipient's email instead
Messages are stored on the sender's device only
quizBank.correctAnswer: Messages are stored in a persistent queue on the server and delivered when the recipient reconnects
quizBank.explanation: When a recipient is offline, the server stores messages in a persistent queue (per-user). When the recipient reconnects, the server delivers all queued messages in order. The server stores messages encrypted (since E2EE means the server can't read them anyway). Messages are deleted from the server after delivery confirmation. If the recipient doesn't come online for 30 days, messages are purged.

quizBank.question: What is the key challenge of end-to-end encryption in group chats?
options: Groups cannot be encrypted
The sender must encrypt the message separately for each group member (N encryptions for N members), or use sender keys where the sender distributes a shared key to all members
Only the group admin can read messages
Group messages are encrypted with a single static key
quizBank.correctAnswer: The sender must encrypt the message separately for each group member (N encryptions for N members), or use sender keys where the sender distributes a shared key to all members
quizBank.explanation: WhatsApp uses the 'sender keys' approach for group E2EE. Each sender generates a sender key and distributes it (via individual E2EE channels) to all group members. Then the sender encrypts the message once with their sender key, and all members can decrypt. When a member leaves, the sender generates a new sender key and redistributes. This is O(1) encryption per message vs O(N) for individual encryption.

quizBank.question: How does WhatsApp maintain message ordering within a conversation?
options: Server timestamps only
Sequence numbers per conversation: each message gets an incrementing sequence number, clients use this to order messages
Messages are always in order because TCP guarantees ordering
Client-side sorting by message content
quizBank.correctAnswer: Sequence numbers per conversation: each message gets an incrementing sequence number, clients use this to order messages
quizBank.explanation: Each conversation has a monotonically increasing sequence counter. When a message is sent, the server assigns the next sequence number. Clients use this to display messages in order. TCP guarantees in-order delivery per connection, but messages may arrive out of order if they're routed through different paths or if one is retransmitted. Server-assigned sequence numbers provide a canonical ordering.

quizBank.question: Design the message routing: User A on Server 1 sends a message to User B on Server 5. How does the system route it?
quizBank.explanation: **Routing approach:** (1) User A sends message to their connected Chat Server 1. (2) Server 1 looks up User B's connection in a distributed routing table (Redis cluster or ZooKeeper). The entry maps user_id -> chat_server_id. (3) Server 1 forwards the message to Chat Server 5 (direct server-to-server communication via internal RPC/message bus). (4) Server 5 delivers the message to User B's WebSocket connection. (5) Server 5 sends a delivery ACK back to Server 1, which forwards it to User A. **If User B is offline:** Server 1 writes the message to User B's persistent queue (Cassandra/custom store), keyed by user_id. When User B reconnects (to any server), that server checks the queue and delivers pending messages. **Routing table update:** When a user connects, their chat server writes user_id -> server_id to the routing table. On disconnect, the entry is removed.

quizBank.question: What is the difference between at-least-once and exactly-once delivery, and which does WhatsApp use?
quizBank.explanation: **At-least-once:** Every message is guaranteed to be delivered, but may arrive more than once (duplicates possible). Achieved by: sender retransmits if no ACK received within timeout. **Exactly-once:** Every message is delivered exactly one time — no loss, no duplicates. True exactly-once is impossible in distributed systems (Two Generals' Problem). **WhatsApp's approach:** At-least-once delivery with client-side deduplication. (1) Each message has a unique message_id (UUID generated by sender). (2) Server retransmits if no delivery ACK received. (3) Client maintains a set of received message_ids and ignores duplicates. (4) This achieves effectively-exactly-once from the user's perspective. The client dedup set only needs to hold IDs for a short window since retransmissions happen within seconds.

cheatSheet:
# Design WhatsApp - Cheat Sheet

## Scale Numbers
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| MAU | 2B+ |
| Messages/day | 100B+ |
| Messages/sec (avg) | ~1.15M |
| Concurrent connections | ~500M |
| Message size (avg) | ~100 bytes (text) |
| Media messages/day | ~7B (images, video, audio) |

## Core Components
- **Gateway** - SSL termination, connection routing
- **Chat Server** - WebSocket connection management (Erlang)
- **Routing Service** - user -> server mapping (Redis)
- **Message Queue** - offline message storage (Cassandra)
- **Media Service** - upload/download media (S3-like)
- **Presence Service** - online/offline/last seen
- **Group Service** - group management, sender keys
- **Push Notification** - APNs/FCM for offline users

## Technology Choices (Real)
| Component | Technology |
|-----------|-----------|
| Backend | Erlang/OTP on BEAM VM |
| Database | Mnesia -> custom Cassandra-like |
| Cache | ETS (Erlang Term Storage) |
| Protocol | Modified XMPP (binary) |
| Encryption | Signal Protocol (Double Ratchet) |
| Media Storage | Blob storage + CDN |
| Connection | WebSocket / MQTT |

## Message Flow
```
Sender -> Chat Server A -> [Routing Table lookup]
         -> Chat Server B -> Recipient (if online)
         -> Message Queue (if offline)
         -> Push Notification (if offline)
```

## ACK Flow (Tick System)
```
1 grey tick:  message -> server ACK
2 grey ticks: server -> recipient device ACK
2 blue ticks: recipient opened chat (read ACK)
```

## E2E Encryption (Signal Protocol)
- Key exchange: X3DH (Extended Triple Diffie-Hellman)
- Message encryption: Double Ratchet (new key per message)
- Group: Sender Keys (O(1) encryption per message)
- Forward secrecy: compromised key can't decrypt past messages

## Interview Tips
1. Start with the message delivery flow (the core feature)
2. Emphasize: WebSocket, message routing, offline queues
3. E2EE is a differentiator — know Signal Protocol basics
4. Erlang/BEAM is the 'why so efficient' answer
5. Deep dive: group messaging or delivery guarantees

sessions.title: Requirements & Back-of-Envelope Estimation
sessions.paretoJustification: Twitter's numbers are well-known and frequently cited in interviews. Knowing them cold shows preparation and enables quick, accurate estimation.
objectives: Define requirements: tweet, follow, timeline, search, trending, notifications
Calculate tweet QPS, timeline read QPS, and fan-out scale
Understand the extreme read-heavy nature (100,000x more reads than writes)

activities.description: List functional requirements and identify the core feature (timeline)
Estimate: 400M MAU, ~200M DAU, 500M tweets/day, avg 300 followers/user
Calculate: 500M tweets * 300 followers = 150B timeline deliveries/day
Compare write QPS (~6K tweets/sec) vs read QPS (~600K timeline loads/sec)

resources.title: System Design Interview - Alex Xu Ch.11

reviewQuestions: How many tweets are posted per day and what is the write QPS?
If 200M users check their timeline 5 times/day, what is the timeline read QPS?
Why is Twitter described as having a fan-out problem?
What is the average tweet size including metadata?
sessions.successCriteria: Can calculate tweet QPS, timeline QPS, and explain the fan-out challenge.
sessions.content:
## Session 1: Requirements & Back-of-Envelope Estimation ```mermaid graph LR U([400M MAU]) --> W[Write Path
~6K tweets/sec] U --> R[Read Path
~600K timeline/sec] W --> FO[Fan-out Service
150B deliveries/day] R --> TC[(Timeline Cache)] ``` ### The Interview Opening The interviewer says: "Design Twitter." Your first move is always to **scope the problem** — never jump straight into architecture. **Candidate:** "Before I begin, let me clarify the scope. Are we designing the full Twitter platform or focusing on specific features?" **Interviewer:** "Focus on the core — tweets, timelines, follow graph, search, and trending." ### Functional Requirements | Feature | Description | Priority | |---------|-------------|----------| | Tweet | Create, delete, like, retweet (280 chars + media) | P0 | | Timeline | Home timeline (tweets from followed users) | P0 | | Follow | Follow/unfollow users, follower/following lists | P0 | | Search | Search tweets and users | P1 | | Trending | Show trending hashtags/topics | P1 | | Notifications | Likes, retweets, mentions, new followers | P2 | ### Non-Functional Requirements - **Availability**: 99.99% — users expect Twitter to always be up - **Latency**: Timeline load < 200ms (p99) - **Consistency**: Eventual consistency is acceptable for timelines (seconds of delay OK) - **Durability**: Tweets must never be lost once acknowledged ### Back-of-Envelope Estimation ```python # Twitter Scale Estimation MAU = 400_000_000 DAU = 200_000_000 # ~50% of MAU # Write side tweets_per_day = 500_000_000 tweet_write_qps = tweets_per_day / 86400 # ~5,787 ~ 6K QPS peak_write_qps = tweet_write_qps * 3 # ~18K QPS during events # Read side timeline_checks_per_user_per_day = 5 timeline_read_qps = (DAU * timeline_checks_per_user_per_day) / 86400 # = 1,000,000,000 / 86400 ~ 12K QPS # But each check loads ~200 tweets, so effective read QPS is much higher # Fan-out estimation avg_followers = 300 fan_out_per_day = tweets_per_day * avg_followers # = 500M * 300 = 150 BILLION timeline deliveries/day fan_out_qps = fan_out_per_day / 86400 # ~1.7M fan-out ops/sec # Storage avg_tweet_size_bytes = 300 # text + metadata (no media) tweet_storage_per_day = tweets_per_day * avg_tweet_size_bytes # = 500M * 300B ~ 150 GB/day ~ 55 TB/year ``` ```java // Java: Quick estimation helper public class TwitterEstimation { static final long MAU = 400_000_000L; static final long DAU = 200_000_000L; static final long TWEETS_PER_DAY = 500_000_000L; static final int AVG_FOLLOWERS = 300; static final int SECONDS_PER_DAY = 86_400; public static void main(String[] args) { long writeQPS = TWEETS_PER_DAY / SECONDS_PER_DAY; long fanOutPerDay = TWEETS_PER_DAY * AVG_FOLLOWERS; long fanOutQPS = fanOutPerDay / SECONDS_PER_DAY; System.out.println("Write QPS: " + writeQPS); // ~6K System.out.println("Fan-out/day: " + fanOutPerDay); // 150B System.out.println("Fan-out QPS: " + fanOutQPS); // ~1.7M } } ``` ### The Read/Write Ratio Twitter is **extremely read-heavy**: for every tweet written, it must be delivered to hundreds of followers. The ratio of fan-out operations to tweet writes is **~300:1**, and the ratio of timeline reads to tweet writes is **~100:1**. > **Interview Tip:** Stating the read/write ratio early signals that you understand what drives architectural decisions. At Twitter's scale, you optimize the read path aggressively — pre-compute timelines, cache everything, and accept eventual consistency. ### Key Insight: The Fan-Out Problem The single most important concept in Twitter system design is the **fan-out problem**. When a user with 10M followers tweets, that single write must be delivered to 10 million timelines. This is the core challenge that drives every major architectural decision.

sessions.title: High-Level Architecture
sessions.paretoJustification: Twitter's architecture evolution (from monolith to microservices) is one of the most documented in tech. Understanding it gives you real-world context for architectural decisions.
objectives: Design the high-level architecture with write and read paths
Understand Twitter's real services: SnowFlake IDs, Manhattan (KV store), Gizzard (sharding)
Identify the fan-out service as the critical component

activities.description: Draw architecture: Client -> CDN -> LB -> API -> Fan-out / Timeline / Search -> Storage
Design write path: tweet -> fan-out service -> push to follower timelines
Design read path: timeline request -> pre-computed timeline cache
Map Twitter's real services to architecture components

reviewQuestions: What is the role of the fan-out service in Twitter's architecture?
Why does Twitter pre-compute timelines instead of computing them on the fly?
What is Manhattan and why did Twitter build their own KV store?
How does the architecture handle both the tweet write path and the timeline read path?
sessions.successCriteria: Can draw a complete architecture with fan-out service and pre-computed timelines.
sessions.content:
## Session 2: High-Level Architecture ```mermaid graph TB Client([Mobile/Web Client]) --> CDN[CDN
Static assets + media] Client --> LB[Load Balancer] LB --> API[API Gateway] API --> TweetSvc[Tweet Service] API --> TimelineSvc[Timeline Service] API --> SearchSvc[Search Service] API --> UserSvc[User Service] TweetSvc --> FanOut[Fan-out Service] FanOut --> TLC[(Timeline Cache
Redis)] TweetSvc --> TweetDB[(Tweet Store
Manhattan)] TimelineSvc --> TLC TimelineSvc --> TweetDB SearchSvc --> SearchIdx[(Search Index
Earlybird)] UserSvc --> UserDB[(User Store
MySQL/Gizzard)] FanOut --> MQ[Message Queue
Kafka] MQ --> SearchIdx MQ --> NotifSvc[Notification Service] ``` ### Twitter's Real Architecture Twitter's architecture evolved from a Ruby on Rails monolith to a complex microservice architecture. Understanding this evolution helps explain *why* certain decisions were made. ### Core Services | Service | Responsibility | Twitter's Real Name | |---------|---------------|-------------------| | ID Generator | Globally unique, time-sorted tweet IDs | Snowflake | | Tweet Store | Store and retrieve tweets | Manhattan | | Timeline Cache | Pre-computed home timelines | Redis clusters | | Fan-out Service | Deliver tweets to follower timelines | Fan-out Service | | Search Index | Real-time tweet search | Earlybird | | User/Social Graph | Follow relationships | FlockDB | | Sharding Layer | Distribute data across MySQL | Gizzard | ### The Write Path When a user posts a tweet: ```python # Simplified write path def post_tweet(user_id: str, tweet_text: str, media_urls: list): # 1. Generate globally unique, time-sortable ID tweet_id = snowflake.generate_id() # 2. Store the tweet tweet = { "id": tweet_id, "user_id": user_id, "text": tweet_text, "media_urls": media_urls, "created_at": timestamp_from_snowflake(tweet_id), } manhattan.put(f"tweets:{tweet_id}", tweet) # 3. Trigger fan-out asynchronously kafka.publish("tweet-created", { "tweet_id": tweet_id, "user_id": user_id, "follower_count": get_follower_count(user_id), }) return tweet_id ``` ```java // Java: Write path public class TweetService { private final SnowflakeIdGenerator idGen; private final ManhattanClient store; private final KafkaProducer kafka; public long postTweet(String userId, String text, List mediaUrls) { // 1. Generate Snowflake ID long tweetId = idGen.nextId(); // 2. Store tweet Tweet tweet = new Tweet(tweetId, userId, text, mediaUrls, Instant.now()); store.put("tweets:" + tweetId, tweet); // 3. Publish for fan-out kafka.send("tweet-created", new TweetEvent(tweetId, userId, getFollowerCount(userId))); return tweetId; } } ``` ### The Read Path When a user opens their timeline: 1. **Request** hits the Timeline Service 2. Timeline Service reads from the **pre-computed timeline cache** (Redis) 3. The cache contains a list of tweet IDs for this user 4. Tweet details are fetched from the **Tweet Store** (Manhattan) 5. Response is assembled and returned ```python def get_home_timeline(user_id: str, cursor: str = None, count: int = 20): # Read pre-computed timeline from Redis tweet_ids = redis.zrevrange( f"timeline:{user_id}", start=cursor or 0, stop=count ) # Batch-fetch tweet objects tweets = manhattan.multi_get([f"tweets:{tid}" for tid in tweet_ids]) # Hydrate with user info, like counts, etc. return hydrate_tweets(tweets) ``` ### Snowflake IDs Twitter's Snowflake generates 64-bit unique IDs that are **time-sortable**: ``` | 1 bit unused | 41 bits timestamp | 10 bits machine ID | 12 bits sequence | ``` - 41 bits of timestamp = ~69 years of unique IDs - 10 bits of machine ID = 1024 machines - 12 bits of sequence = 4096 IDs per machine per millisecond > **Interview Tip:** Mentioning Snowflake IDs by name shows you've studied Twitter specifically. It also solves the "how do you generate unique IDs across distributed systems?" sub-problem elegantly. ### Why Pre-Compute Timelines? Computing a timeline on-the-fly requires: 1. Query the follow graph for all users you follow 2. Fetch recent tweets from each followed user 3. Merge and sort by time 4. Return top N At 600K+ timeline reads/sec, this would be catastrophically expensive. Pre-computing timelines and storing them in Redis reduces read latency from seconds to milliseconds.

reviewQuestions: Why does Twitter use Redis sorted sets for timeline caching?
How is the follows table structured and indexed for fast lookups?
What is the trade-off of storing tweets by tweet_id vs by user_id?
How does Twitter generate globally unique tweet IDs (Snowflake)?

sessions.content:
## Session 3: Database Design & Storage

```mermaid
erDiagram
    USERS {
        bigint user_id PK
        varchar username
        varchar display_name
        text bio
        timestamp created_at
    }
    TWEETS {
        bigint tweet_id PK
        bigint user_id FK
        varchar text
        bigint reply_to_tweet_id
        bigint retweet_of_id
        timestamp created_at
    }
    FOLLOWS {
        bigint follower_id PK
        bigint followee_id PK
        timestamp created_at
    }
    LIKES {
        bigint user_id PK
        bigint tweet_id PK
        timestamp created_at
    }
    USERS ||--o{ TWEETS : posts
    USERS ||--o{ FOLLOWS : follows
    USERS ||--o{ LIKES : likes
```

### Schema Design

#### Tweets Table

```sql
CREATE TABLE tweets (
    tweet_id    BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,  -- Snowflake ID (time-sortable)
    user_id     BIGINT NOT NULL,
    text        VARCHAR(280) NOT NULL,
    reply_to    BIGINT,              -- NULL if not a reply
    retweet_of  BIGINT,              -- NULL if not a retweet
    media_urls  JSON,
    like_count  INT DEFAULT 0,
    retweet_count INT DEFAULT 0,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    INDEX idx_user_time (user_id, created_at DESC),
    INDEX idx_reply (reply_to)
);
```

#### Follows Table (Social Graph)

```sql
CREATE TABLE follows (
    follower_id  BIGINT NOT NULL,
    followee_id  BIGINT NOT NULL,
    created_at   TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (follower_id, followee_id),
    INDEX idx_followee (followee_id, follower_id)
);
-- Sharded by follower_id for "who do I follow?" queries
-- Secondary index on followee_id for "who follows me?" queries
```

### Twitter's Real Storage Systems

| System | Type | Use Case |
|--------|------|----------|
| MySQL + Gizzard | Relational + sharding | Tweets, user data (legacy) |
| Manhattan | Distributed KV store | Tweets, timelines, counters |
| Redis | In-memory cache | Timeline cache, session data |
| HDFS | Distributed file system | Analytics, logs, ML training |
| FlockDB | Graph database | Follow relationships |
| Blobstore | Object storage | Images, videos |

### Timeline Cache Structure (Redis)

Each user's home timeline is stored as a Redis Sorted Set:

```python
# Timeline cache: sorted set of tweet IDs scored by timestamp
# Key: timeline:{user_id}
# Score: tweet timestamp (from Snowflake ID)
# Value: tweet_id

class TimelineCache:
    def __init__(self, redis_client):
        self.redis = redis_client
        self.MAX_TIMELINE_SIZE = 800  # Keep last 800 tweets

    def add_tweet(self, user_id: int, tweet_id: int, timestamp: float):
        key = f"timeline:{user_id}"
        pipe = self.redis.pipeline()
        pipe.zadd(key, {tweet_id: timestamp})
        pipe.zremrangebyrank(key, 0, -(self.MAX_TIMELINE_SIZE + 1))
        pipe.expire(key, 7 * 86400)  # 7-day TTL
        pipe.execute()

    def get_timeline(self, user_id: int, offset: int = 0, count: int = 20):
        key = f"timeline:{user_id}"
        tweet_ids = self.redis.zrevrange(key, offset, offset + count - 1)
        return tweet_ids
```

```java
// Java: Timeline cache with Jedis
public class TimelineCache {
    private static final int MAX_SIZE = 800;
    private static final int TTL_SECONDS = 7 * 86400;
    private final JedisPool jedisPool;

    public void addTweet(long userId, long tweetId, double timestamp) {
        String key = "timeline:" + userId;
        try (Jedis jedis = jedisPool.getResource()) {
            Pipeline pipe = jedis.pipelined();
            pipe.zadd(key, timestamp, String.valueOf(tweetId));
            pipe.zremrangeByRank(key, 0, -(MAX_SIZE + 1));
            pipe.expire(key, TTL_SECONDS);
            pipe.sync();
        }
    }

    public List<Long> getTimeline(long userId, int offset, int count) {
        String key = "timeline:" + userId;
        try (Jedis jedis = jedisPool.getResource()) {
            return jedis.zrevrange(key, offset, offset + count - 1)
                .stream().map(Long::parseLong).collect(Collectors.toList());
        }
    }
}
```

### Sharding Strategy

Twitter shards data primarily by **user_id**:

- **Tweets**: Sharded by `user_id` — all tweets by a user are on the same shard
- **Follows**: Sharded by `follower_id` — fast "who do I follow?" lookups
- **Timeline cache**: Sharded by `user_id` — each user's timeline on one Redis node

> **Interview Tip:** When the interviewer asks about sharding, always explain the **access pattern** that drives the sharding key choice. For Twitter, most queries are "get tweets by user" or "get timeline for user," so `user_id` is the natural shard key.

### Storage Estimation

```python
# Per-tweet storage
tweet_metadata = 8 + 8 + 280 + 8 + 8 + 200 + 4 + 4 + 8  # ~528 bytes
tweets_per_day = 500_000_000
daily_storage = tweets_per_day * tweet_metadata  # ~264 GB/day
yearly_storage = daily_storage * 365  # ~96 TB/year (tweets only)

# Timeline cache (Redis)
timeline_entry = 8  # tweet_id only
entries_per_user = 800
users_with_timelines = 200_000_000  # DAU
timeline_cache_size = users_with_timelines * entries_per_user * timeline_entry
# = 200M * 800 * 8 = 1.28 TB of Redis
```

sessions.content:
## Session 5: Fan-out & Timeline Generation

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Fan-out-on-Write"
        TW1[Tweet Created] --> FOS[Fan-out Service]
        FOS --> TL1[(User A Timeline)]
        FOS --> TL2[(User B Timeline)]
        FOS --> TL3[(User C Timeline)]
        FOS --> TLN[(... 300 followers)]
    end

    subgraph "Fan-out-on-Read"
        TR[Timeline Request] --> QF[Query Follow List]
        QF --> FT1[Fetch User X Tweets]
        QF --> FT2[Fetch User Y Tweets]
        QF --> FT3[Fetch User Z Tweets]
        FT1 --> MG[Merge & Sort]
        FT2 --> MG
        FT3 --> MG
    end
```

### The Core Problem

When a user tweets, how do you deliver that tweet to all their followers' timelines? There are two fundamental approaches.

### Fan-out-on-Write (Push Model)

When a tweet is created, **push** it to every follower's timeline cache immediately.

```python
class FanOutOnWrite:
    def __init__(self, redis, social_graph):
        self.redis = redis
        self.social_graph = social_graph

    def fan_out(self, tweet_id: int, user_id: int, timestamp: float):
        """Push tweet to all follower timelines."""
        followers = self.social_graph.get_followers(user_id)

        # Fan-out to each follower's timeline cache
        pipe = self.redis.pipeline()
        for follower_id in followers:
            key = f"timeline:{follower_id}"
            pipe.zadd(key, {str(tweet_id): timestamp})
            pipe.zremrangebyrank(key, 0, -801)  # Keep max 800
        pipe.execute()
```

**Pros:**
- Timeline reads are **O(1)** — just read from cache
- Read latency is extremely low (~5ms)
- Simple read path

**Cons:**
- Write amplification: 1 tweet to N cache writes (N = follower count)
- Celebrity problem: a celebrity tweeting means 200M+ cache writes
- Wasted work if followers never check their timeline

### Fan-out-on-Read (Pull Model)

When a user requests their timeline, **pull** tweets from all followed users on the fly.

```python
class FanOutOnRead:
    def __init__(self, tweet_store, social_graph):
        self.tweet_store = tweet_store
        self.social_graph = social_graph

    def get_timeline(self, user_id: int, count: int = 20):
        """Pull and merge tweets from all followed users."""
        following = self.social_graph.get_following(user_id)

        # Fetch recent tweets from each followed user
        all_tweets = []
        for followee_id in following:
            tweets = self.tweet_store.get_recent(followee_id, limit=count)
            all_tweets.extend(tweets)

        # Sort by time, return top N
        all_tweets.sort(key=lambda t: t.created_at, reverse=True)
        return all_tweets[:count]
```

**Pros:**
- No write amplification — tweet is stored once
- Celebrity tweets do not cause problems on write
- No wasted work

**Cons:**
- Read latency is **O(F)** where F = number of followed users
- Merging hundreds of user feeds in real-time is slow
- At 600K timeline reads/sec, this does not scale

### Twitter's Hybrid Approach

Twitter uses a **hybrid model** — the critical insight that separates senior candidates:

```python
class HybridFanOut:
    CELEBRITY_THRESHOLD = 10_000  # followers

    def __init__(self, redis, social_graph, tweet_store):
        self.redis = redis
        self.social_graph = social_graph
        self.tweet_store = tweet_store

    def on_tweet_created(self, tweet_id: int, user_id: int, timestamp: float):
        follower_count = self.social_graph.get_follower_count(user_id)

        if follower_count < self.CELEBRITY_THRESHOLD:
            # Regular user: fan-out-on-write (push)
            self._push_to_followers(tweet_id, user_id, timestamp)
        else:
            # Celebrity: skip fan-out, will be pulled on read
            self._store_in_celebrity_tweets(tweet_id, user_id, timestamp)

    def get_timeline(self, user_id: int, count: int = 20):
        # 1. Get pre-computed timeline (from fan-out-on-write)
        cached_tweets = self.redis.zrevrange(f"timeline:{user_id}", 0, count - 1)

        # 2. Get tweets from celebrities the user follows
        celebrity_followees = self.social_graph.get_celebrity_followees(user_id)
        celebrity_tweets = []
        for celeb_id in celebrity_followees:
            tweets = self.tweet_store.get_recent(celeb_id, limit=5)
            celebrity_tweets.extend(tweets)

        # 3. Merge and sort
        all_tweets = list(cached_tweets) + celebrity_tweets
        all_tweets.sort(key=lambda t: t.timestamp, reverse=True)
        return all_tweets[:count]
```

```java
// Java: Hybrid fan-out implementation
public class HybridFanOutService {
    private static final int CELEBRITY_THRESHOLD = 10_000;
    private final JedisPool redis;
    private final SocialGraphService socialGraph;
    private final TweetStore tweetStore;

    public void onTweetCreated(long tweetId, long userId, double timestamp) {
        int followerCount = socialGraph.getFollowerCount(userId);

        if (followerCount < CELEBRITY_THRESHOLD) {
            pushToFollowers(tweetId, userId, timestamp);
        } else {
            // Celebrity: store in dedicated celebrity-tweets list
            tweetStore.addToCelebrityFeed(userId, tweetId, timestamp);
        }
    }

    public List<Tweet> getTimeline(long userId, int count) {
        // Pre-computed timeline
        List<Long> cachedIds = getFromCache(userId, count);

        // Merge with celebrity tweets
        List<Long> celebFollowees = socialGraph.getCelebrityFollowees(userId);
        List<Tweet> merged = new ArrayList<>(hydrate(cachedIds));
        for (long celebId : celebFollowees) {
            merged.addAll(tweetStore.getRecent(celebId, 5));
        }

        merged.sort(Comparator.comparing(Tweet::getTimestamp).reversed());
        return merged.subList(0, Math.min(count, merged.size()));
    }
}
```

### Comparison Table

| Aspect | Fan-out-on-Write | Fan-out-on-Read | Hybrid |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|--------|
| Write latency | High (N writes) | Low (1 write) | Medium |
| Read latency | Low (cache read) | High (N fetches + merge) | Low |
| Celebrity handling | Terrible | Good | Good |
| Storage cost | High (duplicated) | Low | Medium |
| Consistency | Eventual | Real-time | Mixed |
| Used by | Twitter (non-celebs) | Facebook (early) | Twitter (production) |

### The Celebrity Problem in Detail

Consider what happens when a celebrity with 200M followers tweets with pure fan-out-on-write:
- 200 million Redis writes required
- At 100K writes/sec per Redis node, that is ~2000 seconds (33 minutes!)
- Their followers would not see the tweet for half an hour

With the hybrid approach, celebrity tweets are fetched on-read. Since most users follow only a few celebrities, merging 5-10 celebrity feeds at read time adds negligible latency.

> **Interview Tip:** The fan-out-on-write vs fan-out-on-read trade-off is the number one most asked question about Twitter design. Always present the hybrid approach with the celebrity threshold — it shows you understand both the theoretical trade-offs and the practical engineering solution.
